briefing.incidentSummary:
Twelve students at a Mumbai engineering college suddenly show 100% attendance — despite multiple verified absences.

The college attendance portal was accessed and records altered over three nights.

An inside job is suspected.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Identify who altered the attendance records

› Find which credentials were used

› Collect at least 3 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: Tanmay D
suspects.profileNotes: CS student with SQL knowledge. Accessed the attendance database directly using stolen faculty credentials. Charged 500 rupees per student for the service.

suspects.name: Prof. Mehta
suspects.profileNotes: Credentials were stolen from Prof. Mehta's unattended workstation. Professor had no knowledge of the breach.

suspects.name: Rekha P
suspects.profileNotes: Has portal access but no motive. Activity confined to business hours. No nighttime logins from her credentials.

suspects.name: Rohan S
suspects.profileNotes: Was one of the 12 beneficiaries — paid Tanmay for the service. Did not perform the hack himself.

items.label: Need attendance fixed? ₹500 per subject. DM me.
items.detail: Posted in a college WhatsApp group 4 days before the breach. Direct solicitation for the illegal service.

items.label: Done bro. Check portal tomorrow morning.
items.detail: Sent to 12 different students over 3 nights after each batch of changes was made.

items.label: Sending ₹500 now. Change my attendance for Networks subject.
items.detail: Rohan S was a customer — not the perpetrator. Shows demand existed but does not link to the actual breach.

items.label: i can get your attendance sorted. 500 per subject. dm me.
items.detail: Post found on the college's unofficial student forum, timestamped 2 weeks before the breach. The post advertises attendance manipulation for ₹500 per subject. The post account was deleted. However, Tanmay D's modification affected only his own records — not a paid service pattern. Also the modification method used DB-level SQL access, far beyond what a forum tout would offer.
items.irrelevantReason: This is a separate actor offering a different (manual) service. The database-level SQL attack on Tanmay D's records requires internal credential access — not consistent with a paid forum service.

items.label: attendance_db_access_log.txt
items.detail: Database server access log for the college's student information system (SIS-DB-01). Three separate logins were recorded under Prof. Mehta's credential set: 01:14 AM, 01:22 AM, and 01:47 AM. Each session lasted between 4 and 11 minutes. Prof. Mehta's registered login devices are a faculty-issued laptop (IP 10.0.1.44) and his home broadband (IP 49.204.xx.xx). All three 1 AM sessions originated from IP 10.20.33.41 — the hostel block C WiFi gateway, an address not associated with any faculty member. The sessions did not trigger the SIS anomaly alert because the IP was within the college's internal subnet. The credential set was last changed 6 months ago; Prof. Mehta confirmed he has not changed it since.

items.label: executed_queries.sql
items.detail: Database query log showing 36 UPDATE statements executed on the student_attendance table during the breach window. Each query follows the pattern: UPDATE attendance SET status='P' WHERE student_id='TD-2021-047' AND date='[date]'. Student ID TD-2021-047 corresponds to Tanmay D. Dates modified span the last 3 months — converting 'A' (absent) entries to 'P' (present) and 'L' (late) to 'P'. The attendance threshold for exam eligibility at this institution is 75%. Tanmay's pre-modification attendance was 58% — below the threshold. Post-modification: 81% — now eligible. The queries were executed in batches of 12, with a 6-second pause between batches, consistent with a script with a rate-limiting delay rather than manual entry. No other student's records were modified.

hiddenItem.label: hostel_wifi_log.txt
hiddenItem.detail: Hostel network DHCP and session log for Block C WiFi (gateway 10.20.33.41). At 01:11 AM, device hostname 'TANMAY-LAPTOP' was assigned lease 10.20.33.87. The device remained connected until 02:03 AM — covering the entire window of SIS database access. The device's MAC address resolves to an Intel Wi-Fi 6 AX201 adapter — consistent with a mid-range student laptop. Tanmay D's room is in Block C, Room 314, confirmed by hostel admission records. The DHCP lease timestamp predates the first SIS login by 3 minutes, consistent with the time needed to open a laptop, connect to WiFi, and launch the database tool. No other device in Block C was active on the SIS port during this window.

minigame.title: Trace the Breach IP
minigame.hint: One of these IPs accessed the database from the hostel network. Find it.
hints: Look at the subnet — hostel networks use private 10.x.x.x ranges. The gateway will be on 10.20.x.x.
Block C is the third block — that means the third octet is 33. You're looking for 10.20.33.x
The gateway IP ends in .41 — type: 10.20.33.41

onSuccess.message: BREAKTHROUGH — hostel_wifi_log.txt confirmed. TANMAY-LAPTOP was connected from Block C at exactly the time of the database breach. Physical location is now proven.

items.label: Stolen Credential Source
items.detail: Prof. Mehta's credentials were captured via a keylogger installed on the faculty room computer 6 days before the breach. Keylogger file matches software found on Tanmay D's USB drive.

items.label: Intrusion Source IP
items.detail: IP 10.0.1.77 originates from the college hostel network — Block C, Room 312. That room is assigned to Tanmay D.

timeline.title: Keylogger Installed
timeline.description: Keylogger placed on Prof. Mehta's faculty PC

timeline.title: Service Advertised
timeline.description: Tanmay posts offer in college WhatsApp group

timeline.title: Students Verify Changes
timeline.description: 12 students confirm their attendance is now 100%

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Tanmay D exposed as the attendance hacker.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The real culprit was Tanmay D.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

aria.welcomeToHub:
...Twelve students. Perfect attendance. All lies.
Somebody sold shortcuts into the system.
Find who opened that door.
aria.exploreFeeds:
Database logs. SQL queries. Hostel network traces.
The breach happened at night. So did the money.
aria.viewEvidence:
Stolen credentials. Beginner SQL. Hostel IP.
This was an inside job by someone who knew just enough.
aria.decryptionHint:
The hostel network log has the device name encoded.
Decode it.
aria.flagSuspect:
A trace is forming.
You're on to something — keep pulling the thread.
More fragments remain. Don't stop now.
